# Data Analysis for Continuous Improvement: Posting Recorded Lectures 

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Karina Wagner

# Question and Analysis 

Our analysis questioned whether or not students would prefer to have lectures recordings posted to use as a resource for learning material/studying. We analyzed available data about whether students wanted to add a livestream lecture option, wanted pre-lecture videos to watch, thought lesson videos would be effective in helping to learn topics, and the level of understanding respondants had. Since the survey did not directly ask students if they wanted the option of lecture recordings being posted, we cannot definitively say that the data supports our idea. That being said, our analysis on the four markers we thought correlated with our idea do support posting lecture recordings. As seen in the graphs, the majority of respondants chose option 7 (strong agree) when asked if they would like in-person lectures to be live streamed. Additionally, our graphs show that the majority of studnets also selected option 7 (strongly agree) when asked if they think optional pre-lecture videos would help them to prepare for the content of each lecture and would be helpful for their learning. Our data shows that students support some kind of video to complement their in-person lecture learning, which I think is a sign that studnets may also support posting recorded videos of lectures. 
# Extensions and Refinements
In the future, I think it would be interesting to further explore how students choose to study for assessments and what resources they use. If we were to ask if people watch the posted review sessions for each quiz, that may be further indication that students would watch posted lecture recordings to prepare for assessments as well. I reccomend posting lecture recordings after each lecture, and also implementing either PollEverywhere questions or using Carolina Check-In. With lecture recordings, students may feel inclined to skip class because they can use the recording to complete the LS, however, implementing either PollEverywhere, Carolina Check-In, or even both would prevent students from being able to skip class. I support continuing with LS even though they wouldn't be the sole attendance portion of students' grades because I think the LS helped me to recap what I learned in a day, and I found them useful.
